revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:家学]]


'''Kagaku''' (家学) refers to the inheritance of academic knowledge and skills within a family or {{ill|Clan|en|Clan|qid=Q211503|lt=clan|1=Clan}}.
== Overview ==
The inheritance of skills and knowledge through familial or clan-based traditions, known as "'''shushū''' (世習)," was observed in Japanese clans prior to the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato period|1=Yamato period}}. However, under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}, such methods were replaced with education conducted by public institutions such as the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|qid=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō|1=Daigaku-ryō}}, {{ill|Ten'yakuryō|ja|典薬寮|1=Bureau of Pharmacy|2=ja|3=典薬寮|4=fr|5=Bureau de pharmacie|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'yakuryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Bureau of Pharmacy|qq=}}, and {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
Nevertheless, families of instructors (professors) often amassed valuable books and resources at home, which their children could access early. Professors would use these materials for familial education, which often resulted in their children assuming the same official positions if deemed capable. However, the inheritance of official positions through shushū required specific skills to be passed down across generations. Families that could no longer meet these requirements lost their positions. This explains the existence of family lines that once succeeded as professors but later became extinct.
By the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, as the {{ill|Kanshi Chōdai Seido|ja|官司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshi Chōdai Seido|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452090}} evolved, positions such as professor became perceived as rights, leading to increased {{ill|Line of hereditary succession|en|Line of hereditary succession|qid=Q756689|lt=hereditary succession|1=Line of hereditary succession}}. This gave rise to specific clans ("{{ill|Hakaseke|ja|博士家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakaseke|1=Hakaseke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q97175248}}") that exclusively inherited these teaching roles. In fields like {{ill|Myōgyōdō|ja|明経道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōgyōdō|1=myōgyōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11241198}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidendō|1=Kidendō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11604971}}, various families developed their own interpretations of the {{ill|Okototen|ja|乎古登点|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okototen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (annotation system for classical Chinese texts) to create "'''kasei''' (家説)," which were passed down within the family and taught to disciples.
Key examples include:
* '''Kidendō''': {{ill|Sugawara clan|en|Sugawara clan|qid=Q1771125|lt=Sugawara clan|1=Sugawara clan}}, {{ill|Ōe clan|ja|大江氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōe clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Kiyohara clan (Hirozumi line)|ja|清原氏 (広澄流)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara clan (Hirozumi line)|1=Kiyohara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q85882030}}
* '''Myōgyōdō''': {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11363164}}, {{ill|Kiyohara clan|en|Kiyohara clan|qid=Q2975295|lt=Kiyohara clan|1=Kiyohara clan}}
** The families specializing in Myōgyōdō also absorbed disciplines like {{ill|Shodō|ja|書道 (大学寮)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shodō|1=Shodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11515197}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ondō|1=ondō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11664356}}.
* '''Myōhōdō''': {{ill|Imibe clan|ja|惟宗氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imibe clan|1=Koremune clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11492955}}, later {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10928477}} and Nakahara clan.
* '''Sandō''': {{ill|Ie clan|ja|家原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ie clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, later {{ill|Mitsuyoshi clan|ja|三善氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuyoshi clan|1=Miyoshi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11354695}} and {{ill|Ozuki clan|ja|小槻氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ozuki clan|1=Ozuki clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11462133}}.
* '''Onmyōdō''':
** Especially '''Astronomy''': {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō Abe clan)|ja|阿倍氏#陰陽道安倍氏|1=Abe_clan#Other_Abe_families|2=ja|3=阿倍氏#陰陽道安倍氏|lt=Abe clan (Onmyoji)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan (Onmyōdō Abe clan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe_clan#Other_Abe_families|qq=}}
** Especially '''Calendar Studies''': {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}
* '''Medicine''': {{ill|Wake clan|ja|和気氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Tanba clan|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}}
* '''Wakadō''': {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|qid=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan|1=Fujiwara clan}} ({{ill|Mikohidari school|ja|御子左流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikohidari school|1=Mikohidari school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q23996327}})
Notably, even within a single clan, divisions over interpretations could lead to the formation of distinct academic traditions, as seen in the Mikohidari school. Some traditions were closely guarded and passed down through a single successor, while others involved many disciples su11orting the main family line. Over time, the link between inherited ability and succession rights weakened as hereditary succession became entrenched.
During the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|qid=Q12554|lt=Medieval Japan|1=Middle Ages}}, the influence of {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|ja|宋学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neo-Confucianism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宋明理学|1=Neo-Confucianism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q825754}} introduced a focus on the {{ill|Four Books|ja|四書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Books|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Four Books|qid=Q1412581}}, prompting Myōgyōdō to establish the "Four Books of Myōgyōdō." Similarly, the {{ill|Ashikaga Gakkō|en|Ashikaga Gakkō|qid=Q1053852|lt=Ashikaga School|1=Ashikaga Gakkō}} promoted its own interpretation system, called {{ill|Gakkōten|ja|学校点|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakkōten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to maintain its authority in scholarship.
By the late medieval period, ideas like those expressed by {{ill|Zeami Motokiyo|en|Zeami Motokiyo|qid=Q169352|lt=Zeami|1=Zeami Motokiyo}} in his *{{ill|Fūshi Kaden|ja|風姿花伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūshi Kaden|1=Fūshikaden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193468}}* emerged. For instance, "In this art, the family secret is passed down to only one person per generation. Even if one is the sole heir, an incapable person cannot inherit. A family is not a family without succession. Succession makes the family." This perspective prioritized preserving the academic tradition itself, allowing for faithful disciples to inherit even if unrelated by blood. This shift gave rise to the {{ill|Iemoto|en|Iemoto|qid=Q2404873|lt=Iemoto system|1=Iemoto}} in which the family head symbolized the authority of the tradition, using licenses to maintain control over the discipline. While the iemoto system often concealed the lack of ability in unfit successors, it also played a significant role in preserving traditional academic and artistic practices.
In the {{ill|Early modern period|en|Early modern period|qid=Q5308718|lt=Edo period|1=Early modern period}}, the rise of publishing undermined the exclusivity of previously secret knowledge, but the {{ill|bakuhan system|ja|幕藩体制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bakuhan system|1=bakuhan system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11243074}} su11orted hereditary systems even in academic fields. New academic dynasties emerged, such as:
* '''Confucianism''': {{ill|Hayashi clan (Confucian scholars)|en|Hayashi clan (Confucian scholars)|qid=Q2975266|lt=Hayashi clan|1=Hayashi clan (Confucian scholars)}}
* '''Kokugaku''': {{ill|Motoori family|ja|本居氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoori family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* '''Medicine''': {{ill|Kusuri-no-shiro|ja|曲直瀬氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusuri-no-shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Taki family|ja|多紀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki family|1=Taki family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16141794}}
* '''Astronomy''': {{ill|Yasui family|ja|安井氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasui family|1=Yasui family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11450219}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaji family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== References ==
* Anki Kinugasa, "Kagaku," in *Daijiten of Social Sciences 3* (Kashima Kenkyūjo Publishing, 1968), ISBN 978-4-306-09154-2.
* Hiroshi Hosoi, "Kagaku," in *Dictionary of Historical Studies 11: Religion and Learning* (Kōbundō, 2004), ISBN 978-4-335-21041-9.

== See Also ==
* {{ill|Family business|ja|家業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Family business|1=Family business|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17239757}}
* {{ill|Hereditary position|ja|家職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hereditary position|1=Hereditary position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3656910}}
* {{ill|Kaidō|ja|家道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaidō|1=Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11455580}}
* {{ill|Line of hereditary succession|en|Line of hereditary succession|qid=Q756689|lt=Hereditary succession|1=Line of hereditary succession}}
